evidence.reported_evidence: Reported numbers: Of 23 participants, 20 completed their post consultation questionnaires; the remaining three became acutely unwell but gave verbal feedback to the research nurse following the consultation. At 3-week follow-up, 11 patients completed the follow-up questionnaires. Reasons given for non-completion at 3 weeks: five patients were too unwell, four died, two did not respond to a reminder and one patient's carer requested no further involvement. Anxiety scores: reported changes in 19 patients (16 decreased, 2 minimal increase, 1 substantial increase) and overall median decrease reported (median of 8, IQR=1–13).
evidence.missing_information: Whether analyses were intention-to-treat, per-protocol, or other; how missing data were handled in analysis (e.g. excluded, imputed, LOCF); whether participants with only verbal feedback were included in quantitative analyses; whether attrition differed by baseline characteristics or severity.
evidence.low_risk_signals: Reasons for missingness are reported (illness, death, non-response, carer request), which are largely plausibly related to illness severity rather than outcome exposure.
evidence.high_risk_signals: Substantial attrition at 3-week follow-up (12/23 missing) with deaths and illness-related missingness could bias follow-up outcomes; no description of handling missing data or sensitivity analyses.
evidence.outcome_type: Self-reported (questionnaires, anxiety scores), clinician-reported feedback
evidence.attrition_by_arm: Single arm study: Enrolled 23; post-consultation questionnaires completed by 20; 3 gave verbal feedback only. 3-week follow-up completed by 11; missing: 12 (five too unwell, four died, two non-responders, one carer requested no further involvement).
evidence.external_evidence_needed: No for numbers (reported); Yes for analytic approach to missing data and whether those with verbal feedback were included in outcome calculations.

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Participants Three groups felt to have important input were sampled: (a) PC patients, (b) carers of PC patients, and (c) health professionals working in PC. Patients and carers were eligible to take part if they were: (1) over 18 years of age, (2) English speaking, (3) well enough to participate, (4) able to give informed consent, and (5) referred to a specialist PC service and diagnosed with an incurable and progressive illness, or the carer of such a patient. In all, 19 patients and 24 carers were recruited from hospitals, PC units and the community in three PC services in Sydney. ground_truth.support_text: 5 manuscripts in the control group and 4 in the intervention group were lost to follow-up. However, this was before the intervention was delivered and was due to the authors not responding within the deadline. It is unlikely this would have affected the outcome in any meaningful way. After the reviews, 5 manuscripts were rejected in each group. It is unclear if the editors rejecting were blinded or not. However, it is unlikely that this would have had an effect on the result as the rejected manuscripts were of low quality.

domains.domain: Blinding of outcome assessment: All outcomes

evidence.domain: Blinding of outcome assessment: All outcomes
evidence.reported_evidence: "Two evaluators (EC, RD) independently rated the reporting quality ... Both knew the initial and final status but were blinded to the intervention group." "In order to evaluate the success of masking, the evaluators tried to guess which papers had been revised by a statistical reviewer (or with the help of a guideline) ... The blinding process was analyzed and considered successful if the evaluators' hit-proportion was not bigger than that expected by chance (50%). ... On the 'presence of a statistical reviewer' question ... the match percentage was 60.8% (95% CI from 49.1 to 71.6%). On the use of a checklist ... 65.3% (95% CI from 53.5 to 76.0%)."
evidence.missing_information: Although evaluators were intended to be blinded, masking appears imperfect: reported guess match percentages exceed 50% for both interventions. No detail on whether other outcome assessors (e.g., coders, adjudicators) existed. No information on whether evaluators had access to reviewer comments or tracked changes that could unblind them.
evidence.low_risk_signals: Explicit statement that outcome assessors were blinded and that masking success was formally assessed; sensitivity analyses stratifying by response to blinding question retained main conclusions.
evidence.high_risk_signals: Empirical evidence that blinding was imperfect: guess rates 60.8% and 65.3% suggest potential unblinding that could bias assessment.
evidence.outcome_type: Coder-rated / evaluator-rated (MQAI ratings by two independent evaluators)
evidence.external_evidence_needed: No

evidence.reported_evidence: "Of the 129 randomized manuscripts, 14 were lost of follow up because authors missed the deadline and the masked allocation was revealed; ... On the other hand, 16 were rejected by the editorial team after evaluating peer-review reports." "Three different populations were considered: 'complete' which included all randomized manuscripts not lost to follow-up, ... two other populations were defined for the sensitivity analyses: one taking into consideration all 'randomized' manuscripts and one including only those manuscripts accepted for publication. Those manuscripts rejected ... and those lost to follow-up were analyzed considering two different values for their final quality: 1) the initial overall quality was imputed as the final overall quality ... or 2) the final overall quality was assigned a value equal to the mean final quality value of the final versions of the received articles." "The 14 papers lost to follow-up had a heterogeneous distribution among the randomized groups (figure 1) but did not differ, in terms of baseline quality, from the originals in the accepted manuscripts population. We performed several sensitivity analyses including those papers with different imputed values for the final version that produced very similar conclusions."
evidence.missing_information: Exact numbers analyzed per randomized group (i.e., numbers missing/analyzed by arm) not provided in text (figure 1 referenced but not supplied). Reasons for loss to follow-up summarized (authors missed deadline and masked allocation revealed) but not broken down by arm in the text. Whether analyses were conducted as ITT (all randomized) or per-protocol is described but not fully detailed; primary analysis used 'complete' population (all randomized manuscripts not lost to follow-up). Details of statistical handling (e.g., which imputation used for main analysis) and whether LOCF or other methods used beyond the two described imputations not specified.
evidence.low_risk_signals: Authors acknowledged missing data and performed sensitivity analyses with two different imputation strategies; reported that conclusions were robust to these analyses. Reported that lost to follow-up manuscripts did not differ in baseline quality from analyzed manuscripts.
evidence.high_risk_signals: Primary analysis used 'complete' population excluding 14 lost to follow-up (i.e., not strict ITT); missing manuscripts had heterogeneous distribution among groups which could bias results if not fully accounted for; exact distribution by arm not reported in the text provided.
evidence.outcome_type: Coder-rated / evaluator-rated (primary outcome: change in MQAI score)
evidence.attrition_by_arm: Overall: 129 randomized, 14 lost to follow-up (authors missed deadlines and allocation revealed), 16 rejected after peer review; 115 analyzed in main "complete" analysis. Arm-level missing numbers not provided in text (figure 1 referenced).
evidence.external_evidence_needed: Yes - need figure 1 or appendix to obtain arm-level attrition and detailed numbers and confirm which imputation was primary

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Introduction Despite being widely accepted as the best way to filter low-quality research, to detect flaws in scientific communications and to improve papers with significant contributions to their fields [1]–[3], peer review has also raised many criticisms [4]–[6]. Certainly, as a process carried out by humans it has its weaknesses and therefore many initiatives have been developed to improve it. For instance, some methodological mistakes are continually repeated in published papers and proposals that referees fail to detect or do not consider properly, and because of this, both the development of reporting guidelines [7]–[11] and the suggestion of adding methodological experts to the referees panel have been promoted [12] and implemented [13]. Accordingly, from what has been said, we might have expected the existence of strong scientific evidence in favor of peer review, but...

evidence.reported_evidence: Data were analysed by intention-to-treat (ITT) analysis whereby all randomised women were included regardless of whether or not they had received the intervention and the number of doses. Figure 1 shows the trial profile and table 1 shows the baseline characteristics of participants. Reported outcome counts in text: e.g., peripheral parasitaemia 35/493 (SP) vs 75/495 (placebo); overall clinical malaria 7% (two-dose SP) and 10% (placebo); other outcome events (infant deaths: 6 in SP, 11 in placebo).
evidence.missing_information: Full CONSORT flow numbers by arm for randomized, received intervention, lost to follow-up, withdrawn, excluded from analysis, and reasons; exact numbers randomized per arm (implied by denominators but not explicit randomised counts), numbers analyzed for each outcome and numbers with missing outcome data by arm and timing; details of how ITT was implemented (e.g., were outcomes sought for all randomized, any imputation used) and whether any sensitivity analyses for missing data performed.
evidence.low_risk_signals: Stated intention-to-treat analysis including all randomized women; outcome denominators provided for several endpoints (e.g., 493 and 495), implying high follow-up for those outcomes.
evidence.high_risk_signals: No detailed attrition table provided in text; absence of explicit counts of losses and reasons limits assessment; potential selective missingness not described.
evidence.outcome_type: All outcomes
evidence.attrition_by_arm: Partially reported in results by outcome (e.g., peripheral parasitaemia 35/493 vs 75/495) but full missingness and attrition flow not provided in text; trial profile (Figure 1) referenced but not included here.
evidence.external_evidence_needed: Yes - full CONSORT flow diagram and per-arm attrition numbers from Figure 1 / protocol or supplementary materials to confirm numbers randomized, lost, and how missing data were handled.

This information should help guide policy towards the rational use of control tools for malaria prevention in pregnancy. --- ## Methods The protocol for this trial and supporting CONSORT checklist are available as supporting information; see Checklist S1 and Protocol S1. --- ## Study design This double blind, individually randomised, placebo controlled trial had the primary objective of estimating the additive protective effect of two-dose IPTp with SP to that of ITNs on LBW prevalence. Based on previous estimates in the area, a LBW prevalence of 20% and an...

evidence.reported_evidence: Participant flow: "Figure 1 illustrates the number of primary care practices randomized and the flow of patients with DM in each study arm." Reported use rates and enrollment: "Use of the parent PG secure Web portal by patients with DM ranged from 7% to 14% of each practice population. Among patients with active PG accounts, the rate of consent to enroll in the advanced patient portal clinical trial was 39% in the DM PHR intervention arm and 35% in the family history and health maintenance PHR active control arm." For analyses of active account users: intervention arm (n=126) and controls (n=118) for HbA1c comparisons; other subgroup numbers: intervention arm users submitting DM PHR Care Plans n=82; control arm submitting journals n=41. Follow-up ascertainment completed March 22, 2007. Primary outcomes analyzed using intention-to-treat: "comparing all eligible patients with DM in the 2 study arms in an intention-to-treat analysis."
evidence.missing_information: Exact numbers randomized (total patients/practices) and numbers analyzed for primary outcomes by arm are not explicitly given in text (Figure 1 not provided); numbers missing and reasons for missing outcome data by arm are not detailed; timing and amount of missing data for each outcome (HbA1c, BP, LDL-C) not reported; methods for handling missing data (e.g., LOCF, multiple imputation) not reported; whether ITT was complete-case or included imputation not clarified.
evidence.low_risk_signals: Authors state primary analyses were intention-to-treat comparing all eligible patients in the two arms; follow-up ascertainment completed and outcomes were collected from electronic records which may reduce loss to follow-up compared with active contact.
evidence.high_risk_signals: Selective reporting of subgroup analyses among 'active account users' with specific Ns suggests differential participation; enrollment/consent rates differ and overall low portal use (7-14%) may lead to substantial missingness from eligible population; lack of detailed attrition numbers and handling methods impedes assessment of bias from incomplete data.
evidence.outcome_type: Lab/physiologic (HbA1c, LDL-C, blood pressure) and clinician behavior/process outcomes (medication intensification).
evidence.attrition_by_arm: Reported subgroup Ns: active account users analyzed: intervention n=126, control n=118; PHR Care Plan submitters: intervention n=82, control journal submitters n=41. Consent rates among active PG accounts: 39% intervention vs 35% control. Exact numbers randomized/enrolled/analyzed/missing overall and by arm not reported in text (Figure 1 referenced but not provided).
evidence.external_evidence_needed: Yes — Figure 1 and full participant flow numbers, per-arm counts randomized and analyzed, reasons for missing data, and details of missing data handling (imputation, sensitivity analyses) are needed from the article's figures, protocol, or author correspondence.

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Front Despite the demonstrated efficacy of medical therapy in controlled clinical trials,1-5 most patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus (DM) cared for in the community do not reach recommended treatment goals for glycemic, blood pressure, or low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) control.6,7 Fundamental changes in how DM care is delivered may be needed to overcome the current “quality chasm” seen in DM management.8 --- ## Front Increasing patients' knowledge about their current risk factor levels9,10 and facilitating collaboration between patients and physicians11-13 have both been demonstrated to improve clinical outcomes. Personal health records (PHRs) that enable patients to interact directly with their own clinical records represent one innovative means to achieve these goals.14–16 Older studies have used patient-carried paper reminder cards and mini records17,18 to engage...

evidence.reported_evidence: Participant flow: "Between May 2003 and October 2004 we invited 246 patients to participate, of whom 94 declined (uptake rate of 62.6%). ... 152 participants were randomised to either the intervention or the control condition. The final sample was 111 patients, of whom 55 were randomly assigned to the intervention group and 56 to the control group. Here, 13 and 16 patients respectively were excluded from the analyses because of post hoc ascertainment of not having met the inclusion criteria (benign diagnosis, carcinoma in situ, Stage IV-tumour)." Follow-up: assessments at baseline and 1 week; follow-up questionnaires were returned by mail using pre-paid envelope. No other missing data counts reported.
evidence.missing_information: Exact numbers analyzed per outcome (e.g., for DCS, PICS) not explicitly stated; numbers lost to follow-up or non-responders at 1-week by arm are not provided; reasons for drop-out after randomisation beyond post hoc exclusions not detailed; whether analysis was intention-to-treat, per-protocol, or handling of missing data (imputation/LOCF) not reported.
evidence.low_risk_signals: Pre-randomisation baseline questionnaire completed prior to randomisation ensures baseline data present; explicit reporting of invited, declined, randomized and exclusions after randomisation provided (numbers given).
evidence.high_risk_signals: Relatively large proportion excluded post-randomisation (13 and 16) for not meeting inclusion criteria — could bias results; final analyzed numbers unclear (says final sample 111 with 55 and 56 but exclusions sum to 29 suggesting attrition unclear); lack of explicit ITT or missing data handling increases risk of bias from incomplete outcome data.
evidence.outcome_type: All outcomes (self-reported scales and chart-extracted uptake rates) — attrition may affect both.
evidence.attrition_by_arm: Reported: Of 152 randomised, final sample 111 with 55 in intervention and 56 in control. Post hoc exclusions: 13 (intervention) and 16 (control) excluded for not meeting inclusion criteria (benign diagnosis, carcinoma in situ, Stage IV). No further numbers of non-responders at follow-up, or numbers analyzed per outcome, are reported.
evidence.external_evidence_needed: Yes — need detailed CONSORT flow (numbers assessed at each stage, numbers providing follow-up data per outcome by arm), analysis population (ITT vs per-protocol), and how missing data were handled.

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Front Although several studies explored the effect of decision aid interventions on treatment decision making as a proxy for the effectiveness of the intervention, we believe that patient attitudes toward the decision making process may be a more important indicator of shared decision making effectiveness as the ultimate treatment choice may depend on several individual factors. Of these, decisional conflict has been demonstrated to be a sensitive measure of decision aid effectiveness (O'Connor et al, 2003). --- ## Front To date, all randomised controlled trials in women with breast cancer have been conducted in North America. Therefore, the aim of this study was to examine the impact of a decision aid intervention in breast cancer inpatients in Germany. Because of the predominance of the inpatient treatment setting, we posited that decision aid interventions may have different, and...

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Background There are well recognised gaps in the implementation of best clinical practice in acute stroke care [1,2]. Important among these are the acute management of physiological variables known to influence stroke outcome. Elevation of blood glucose and body temperature in the early post-stroke period are associated with significantly worse stroke outcomes [3-8]. Management of swallowing dysfunction (dysphagia) also is crucial [9-11]. One of the greatest risks following stroke for a patient with a swallowing abnormality is aspiration which will lead to chest infections, aspiration pneumonia and death [12,13]. National guidelines affirm the importance of personnel on the clinical team specifically trained in swallowing screening as well as professional expertise in therapy and management [14]. Optimal management of these three clinical issues, namely fever, blood sugar, and...

evidence.reported_evidence: Analysis of data was on an intention-to-treat basis [35]. All subjects, who completed the intervention or not, were followed up over a 12-month post-intervention period. Thirty-one subjects (91.2%) from the mutual psychoeducation and support group completed the program. These subjects, together with those who dropped out or absented in more than four group sessions - psycho-education group (n = 3) and routine care group (n = 2), were evaluated at three times of outcome measurements. Reasons for dropout or discontinuation from the group interventions were mainly: insufficient time to attend (n = 2), patient’s mental state worsened (n = 3), not interested (n = 2), and the only person taking care of patient (n = 2).
evidence.missing_information: Exact numbers randomized and analyzed per arm at each timepoint beyond completion numbers; breakdown of missing outcome data by arm for each outcome and timepoint; how missing data were handled in analyses (e.g. last observation carried forward, imputation methods) beyond the general ITT statement; whether outcomes from hospital records were available for all randomized participants; exact mapping of reasons to arms is incomplete (reasons list not fully allocated to arms).

evidence.high_risk_signals: Small number of dropouts (3 intervention, 2 control) but no clear reporting of whether outcome data were missing for these participants at each timepoint or how missing questionnaire data were handled; no description of sensitivity analyses for missing data.
evidence.outcome_type: Applies to all outcomes (self-reported questionnaires and hospital admission data).

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Models of Family Intervention in Schizophrenia Because of changes in the organization of mental health services in both Western and Asian countries [4,16], the past decade has witnessed a substantial increase in demands for family interventions within the community as well as a rapid growth of a variety of family intervention models, which have been influenced by behavioral and cognitive psychological therapies [13]. All family intervention programs offer psycho-education and psychosocial support to family members, and some include the patient, although the theoretical orientation of these interventions varies considerably. Common elements in psycho-educational group interventions include the involvement of the family in: (i) patient care provision, (ii) presentation of information about the mental illness and its management, (iii) discussion of the techniques for patient care such...

evidence.reported_evidence: “149 were randomized to receive citalopram (n=73) or placebo (n=76). Most participants (82.6%) completed the 12-week trial. In the citalopram-treated group, 12.3% stopped early due to adverse events and another 2.7% withdrew consent; in the placebo group, 9.2% left early owing to adverse events and another 6.6% withdrew consent (Figure 1).” “All efficacy and safety analyses used the intent-to-treat principle.” “For subjects who failed to complete all postrandomization assessments, the last observation was carried forward. For comparison purposes, analyses were also performed among subjects who completed all assessments.” “The zero value [drug level] was obtained from a child who dropped out of the study early in the trial but returned for his scheduled 12-week visit, consistent with intent-to-treat principles.”
evidence.missing_information: Exact numbers analyzed for each outcome (per ITT dataset) not provided in text (eg, number with LOCF imputed data for primary outcome). Reasons for missingness beyond adverse events/withdrawal are not fully detailed; timing of withdrawals relative to assessments not fully specified. No detailed sensitivity analyses beyond complete case comparison described (no description of how many had LOCF imputations). Figure 1 referenced but not available in text.
evidence.low_risk_signals: Use of intent-to-treat principle for all efficacy and safety analyses; explicit report of proportions completing the trial (82.6% overall); LOCF specified for missing postrandomization assessments; complete case analyses were performed and were consistent with primary findings.
evidence.high_risk_signals: Differential reasons for withdrawal by arm (eg, higher early stopping for adverse events in active arm) could bias outcomes; LOCF can introduce bias depending on missingness mechanism; lack of detailed numbers analyzed with LOCF and missing data patterns limits assessment.
evidence.outcome_type: All outcomes (clinician-rated, parent-reported, lab measures) as reported.
evidence.attrition_by_arm: Randomized: citalopram n=73, placebo n=76. Completion rate overall 82.6%. Citalopram: 12.3% stopped early due to adverse events (~9 subjects), 2.7% withdrew consent (~2 subjects). Placebo: 9.2% left early due to adverse events (~7 subjects), 6.6% withdrew consent (~5 subjects). Precise counts inferred from percentages but not explicitly enumerated in text. Figure 1 (not provided) purportedly details disposition.
evidence.external_evidence_needed: Yes - exact participant flow numbers by arm (from Figure 1 or CONSORT flow), numbers included in each analysis, and details of timing/reasons for missingness would be needed from the protocol/figure or author correspondence.

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Front Preliminary experience with the serotonergic tricyclic antidepressant clomipramine hydrochloride showed moderate benefit for anger and ritualized behaviors compared with the noradrenergic desipramine hydrochloride.11 However, a subsequent trial comparing clomipramine, the antipsychotic haloperidol, and placebo in adults with ASDs showed no differentiation between clomipramine use and placebo and a high rate of adverse events.12 The SSRI fluvoxamine maleate was superior to placebo for repetitive behavior, aggression, and social relatedness in adults with autism.13 However, in a placebo-controlled study14 of the same medication in children, only 1 of 18 subjects demonstrated improvement on active drug; furthermore, adverse events, including hyperactivity, insomnia, agitation, and aggression, occurred in 14 of 18 children treated with fluvoxamine, suggesting unusual sensitivity of...

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Front To further investigate the effectiveness of the TM program on BP, psychological distress, and coping, we conducted a randomized controlled trial (RCT) in college students. To our knowledge, this is the first RCT to evaluate the efficacy of a mind–body intervention on BP and psychological factors in young adults. --- ## Study design This was a single-blind RCT comparing stress reduction with the TM program to wait-list controls on change in BP, psychological distress, and coping in young adults. The present study reports on both the overall sample and a subgroup at risk for hypertension. The field site was American University (Washington, DC). The administrative and data-coordinating center was the Center for Natural Medicine and Prevention at Maharishi University of Management Research Institute (Maharishi Vedic City, IA). The institutional review boards of both institutions...

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Background Intimate partner abuse (IPA) or violence is defined as any behaviour within an intimate relationship that causes physical, psychological or sexual harm to those in the relationship [1]. Behaviours include acts of physical aggression such as slapping and kicking; psychological abuse such as intimidation and humiliation; forced intercourse and other forms of sexual coercion; and various controlling behaviours such as isolating a person from their family and friends, monitoring their movements, and restricting access to information or assistance. IPA sits within the broader context of gendered violence and the majority of assaults by partners are directed at females [1,2]. Moreover, sexual abuse and partner violence resulting in significant injury are much more commonly perpetrated against women by their partners than against men [3]. Partner abuse is a major public health...

domains.domain: Blinding of outcome assessment: All outcomes

evidence.domain: Blinding of outcome assessment: All outcomes
evidence.reported_evidence: No explicit statement about blinding of outcome assessors. Measures included knowledge inventory (multiple-choice), vignette-based likelihood to report (self-report Likert), and ratings of clinical expertise in reporting (developed with expert consensus). Some measures involved expert review in development/validation (e.g., CPS professionals validated vignettes), but no info about who scored postintervention outcomes or whether those raters were blinded to allocation.
evidence.missing_information: Whether those scoring or rating outcomes (e.g., clinical expertise ratings, any coder/assessor scoring) were blinded to participant allocation; whether self-report measures were collected blinded to hypothesis or allocation; whether objective scoring (multiple choice) was automated or blinded.
evidence.low_risk_signals: Some outcomes (multiple-choice knowledge test and Likert responses) are less susceptible to assessor bias if scored objectively; the knowledge test has objectively correct answers.
evidence.high_risk_signals: No evidence that expert raters assessing clinical expertise or role-play performance were blinded; this could introduce detection bias for subjective outcomes.
evidence.outcome_type: Mix: objective (knowledge multiple-choice), self-reported (likelihood to report on vignettes), and rater-assessed/clinician-rated (clinical expertise/performance in role-play scenarios).

evidence.reported_evidence: "The participants were 55 mental health professionals... One participant was excluded from the study because this individual was unable to complete the postworkshop assessment." "A total of 120 physicians and 172 additional staff completed pre and postviewing questionnaires" (this latter quote is about a different study, McCauley et al., 2003). Also: "Results indicated improvements... However, 60% of the participants did not complete the postintervention assessment." (refers to Reiniger et al., 1995). For the current trial, only the single exclusion of one participant for inability to complete postworkshop assessment is reported. "Chi-square and analyses of variance (ANOVAs) indicated there were no statistical differences between participants in the two experimental conditions prior to receiving training (all p values > .05)."
evidence.missing_information: Numbers randomized to each arm (how the 55 were allocated across the two conditions) not reported; numbers analyzed by arm not reported; timing of missingness beyond 'postworkshop' not detailed; reasons for missingness for the one excluded participant not detailed beyond 'unable to complete the postworkshop assessment'; whether analysis was intention-to-treat, per-protocol, or as-treated not reported; any imputation methods (LOCF, multiple imputation) not reported; whether sensitivity analyses were performed not reported.
evidence.low_risk_signals: Very low overall attrition reported for this study (one participant excluded); report states baseline equivalence between groups.
evidence.high_risk_signals: Lack of arm-specific attrition data and lack of description of analysis population or handling of missing data; exclusion of one participant after randomization might affect balance depending on which arm and reasons.
evidence.outcome_type: All outcomes
evidence.attrition_by_arm: Not reported by arm. Overall: 55 participants enrolled; 1 participant excluded for inability to complete postworkshop assessment. No further breakdown provided.
evidence.external_evidence_needed: Yes - need allocation numbers by arm, analyzed sample sizes by arm, analysis approach (ITT vs per-protocol), and any imputation details from trial protocol or author correspondence.

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Front The current article reports the development of a training program to assist professionals in effectively reporting suspected child maltreatment. Along these lines, we first underscore child maltreatment reporting practices and techniques that have been promoted based on clinical lore or results found in uncontrolled trials (i.e., did not involve random assignment of participants to experimental conditions or case trials involving multiple baseline or withdrawal experimental designs). We then review a training program that is specific to child maltreatment reporting and found to be efficacious in a single-case multiple baseline trial. This program incorporates many of the practices and techniques that have been pioneered in uncontrolled trials. However, as will be carefully reviewed below, this program lacks important features that are relevant to dissemination, such as visual...

Comment: Insufficient information to decide; outcomes include objective tests but also subjective rater-assessed measures with no blinding details.

support_context.quote: No explicit statement about blinding of outcome assessors. ... no info about who scored postintervention outcomes or whether those raters were blinded to allocation.
support_context.relevance: Directly indicates lack of reported information on assessor blinding, which is central to detection bias judgement.

support_context.quote: Outcome_type: Mix: objective (knowledge multiple-choice), self-reported (likelihood to report), and rater-assessed (clinical expertise/performance).
support_context.relevance: Shows presence of subjective rater-assessed outcomes vulnerable to detection bias if assessors were not blinded.

domain_rows.reasoning: Because the report gives no information on whether outcome assessors (especially for subjective rater-assessed measures) were blinded, and several outcomes are susceptible to assessor influence, there is insufficient detail to judge Low or High risk.
ground_truth_support: Comment:detection bias due to knowledge of the allocated intervention by outcome assessors, and outcome measurement is likely to be influenced by lack of blinding (pre‐post self‐report measures tied closely to intervention purpose)

domain_rows.support_text: Quote: "The participants were 55 mental health professionals... One participant was excluded from the study because this individual was unable to complete the postworkshop assessment." Comment: Only a single exclusion is reported but numbers randomized and analyzed by arm, reasons by arm, and methods for handling missing data (ITT/imputation) are not reported, so risk of attrition bias cannot be judged.

support_context.quote: "55 participants... One participant was excluded... unable to complete the postworkshop assessment."
support_context.relevance: Shows the amount and reason for the single reported exclusion but does not give arm-specific or analysis details.

support_context.quote: "Numbers randomized to each arm... not reported; numbers analyzed by arm not reported... whether analysis was intention-to-treat... not reported."
support_context.relevance: Documents key missing information needed to assess whether attrition could bias outcomes.

domain_rows.reasoning: Although overall attrition appears minimal (one exclusion), essential details are missing (allocation by arm, analyzed sample sizes, handling/imputation, and whether ITT was used). Per the criteria, insufficient reporting warrants an Unclear risk judgement.
ground_truth_support: Comment:1 participant was excluded from analyses due to inability to complete the postintervention measure; however, authors do not report the participant's allocation condition (Alvarez 2010, p 212)

assessment.support_text: Quote: "Those who agreed to participate were randomized to either the Standard Treatment group (STD; n=49) or to the Standard Treatment + Cognitive-Behavioral Treatment group (STD+CBT; n=52) using a computerized urn randomization procedure [28]." Comment: A computerized urn randomization implies a random process, but key details about the algorithm, who implemented it, seed/control, and independence are not reported, so adequacy of sequence generation cannot be confirmed.

support_context.quote: "...randomized...using a computerized urn randomization procedure [28]."
support_context.relevance: States the use of a computerized urn method, indicating a randomized component but without procedural details.

support_context.quote: Missing: details on urn parameters, seed, who implemented the computerized procedure, timing relative to enrollment.
support_context.relevance: Highlights absence of information needed to judge whether sequence generation was robust and unpredictable.

support_context.quote: "The Project Coordinator entered the urn data during the intake session and informed the participants of their treatment assignments."
support_context.relevance: Direct statement of how assignments were made and communicated, relevant to whether allocation was concealed before enrolment.

support_context.quote: No information on who controlled assignment before enrolment ... whether Project Coordinator was independent of enrolment or could influence assignment.

assessment.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel: All outcomes
assessment.support_text: Quote: "Participants were informed of their treatment assignments (Project Coordinator \"informed the participants of their treatment assignments\")." support_context.quote: Participants were informed of their treatment assignments (Project Coordinator "informed the participants of their treatment assignments").
support_context.relevance: Shows participants were told their allocation, making self-reported outcomes susceptible to bias.

support_context.quote: Therapists: "The same therapists provided both of the study treatments..."; therapists were not blinded.

assessment.domain: Blinding of outcome assessment: All outcomes
assessment.support_text: Quote: "A trained M.A.-level research associate, who was not blinded to treatment condition, conducted the pretreatment and follow-up research assessments." Comment: An unblinded assessor conducted outcome assessments including self-report questionnaires and possible interviewer-administered measures, creating a risk that knowledge of allocation influenced outcome measurement.

support_context.quote: A trained M.A.-level research associate, who was not blinded to treatment condition, conducted the pretreatment and follow-up research assessments.
support_context.relevance: Direct evidence that the outcome assessor was not blinded, which can bias assessment especially for interviewer-involved or expectation-sensitive outcomes.

support_context.quote: Outcomes included self-report measures (MPI pain, CES-D, others); possible interviewer-administered follow-ups (research assessments) which were unblinded.
support_context.relevance: Shows outcomes were self-reported and potentially influenced by assessor interaction or participant expectations, increasing detection bias risk.

assessment.support_text: Quote: "At posttreatment 88% of patients provided data, and 73% provided data at 52 weeks. Losses to follow-up were equivalent across treatment conditions." evidence.missing_information: No details on the urn algorithm parameters, seed, who implemented the computerized procedure, timing of randomization relative to enrollment beyond 'during the intake session' (see Allocation concealment), or whether allocation sequence was generated by an independent party.

evidence.outcome_type: Not outcome-specific (applies to trial allocation)

evidence.reported_evidence: “Of 196 persons screened, 121 were deemed eligible for the study, and 101 were assigned to treatment. At posttreatment 88% of patients provided data, and 73% provided data at 52 weeks. Losses to follow-up were equivalent across treatment conditions.” Analyses: “intent-to-treat approach... mixed model regression... uses a maximum likelihood estimation procedure to estimate the parameters... takes advantage of all available data.” Also: “Eighty-seven percent of patients attended six treatment sessions, with no significant differences in attendance by treatment condition.”
evidence.missing_information: No explicit numbers of participants assessed/missing by arm at each timepoint (posttreatment and 52 weeks). No reasons for missing data by arm. No description of whether dropouts differed by baseline characteristics. No description of whether any imputation beyond mixed model ML was performed (e.g., LOCF) or of sensitivity analyses for missing data assumptions.
evidence.low_risk_signals: Overall retention reported (88% posttreatment, 73% at 52 weeks). Authors used mixed model (ML) with intent-to-treat which uses all available data and can handle missing-at-random data without ad-hoc imputation. Statements that losses were equivalent across treatment conditions and attendance rates had no significant differences by condition.
evidence.high_risk_signals: Absence of per-arm missing counts and reasons limits assessment of bias from missing data; 27% missing at 52 weeks is substantial and potential for bias if not missing at random.
evidence.outcome_type: Self-reported outcomes (pain, depression, interference) — incomplete data pertains to these
evidence.attrition_by_arm: Not reported by arm. Overall: randomized N=101; overall completion: 88% at posttreatment (~89 participants), 73% at 52 weeks (~74 participants) — arm-specific numbers and reasons not given.
evidence.external_evidence_needed: Yes — per-arm counts of missing participants at each follow-up, reasons for missingness, baseline comparisons of completers vs dropouts, and any sensitivity analyses or protocol-specified missing-data handling

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Front Cognitive-behavioral therapies (CBT) have demonstrated some success in the treatment of pain related to temporomandibular joint dysfunction (TMD) [6; 8; 33], but few of these studies included systematic, active comparison conditions. Thus there is relatively little evidence regarding the long-term effects of CBT compared to active alternatives, or whether CBT works equally well for all patients. --- ## Front Turk and colleagues [29] hypothesized that patients who report emotional and physical difficulties would benefit more from a treatment that included cognitive therapy than from a similar comparison treatment that did not. Only those patients who received the treatment that included cognitive therapy demonstrated continued improvements out to 6 months. The authors concluded that cognitive therapy was effective for highly distressed TMD patients. It is not clear, however,...

evidence_table.study_design: Randomized controlled trial (parallel groups), two-arm (STD n=49 vs STD+CBT n=52), randomized using computerized urn randomization; assessments at baseline, 6, 12, 24, 36, 52 weeks; intent-to-treat analyses using mixed model (maximum likelihood)
evidence_table.notes: All evidence extracted from provided article text only. No external or review-level context was used.

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Introduction Off-pump coronary artery bypass surgery (OPCAB) is widely performed for its benefit in reducing complications from cardiopulmonary bypass [1-3]. However, since the surgical method moves the pumping heart and limits movement of the surgery site with a tissue stabilizer, it can cause serious hemodynamic changes [4,5]. For example, the function of both ventricles declines due to pressure on the heart during OPCAB via diastolic dysfunction of the right ventricle due to pressure [6,7]. Milrinone, a phosphodiesterase III inhibitor, prevents degradation of cyclic adenosine monophosphate in cardiac myocytes to increase contractile force of the heart muscle, and it relaxes the vascular smooth muscle to reduce systemic vascular resistance [8]. Milrinone helps patients avoid cardiopulmonary bypass by increasing heart function. It also enhances cardiac contractility while not...

evidence.reported_evidence: Two hundred ten children ... were randomly assigned. As Figure 1 displays, 16 (8%) children and their parents never returned after being assigned to treatment, and 15 (7%) children and their parents left after attending only one or two sessions. These 31 (15%) children and their parents were defined as dropouts. Therefore, 179 (85%) children attended at least three TF-CBT sessions. This sample of completers was used for the subsequent analyses. ... However, we did run analyses in which all of the patients volunteering for the study were included (intent-to-treat) and compared the results from these analyses with those in which only patients with 3 or more completed treatment sessions were included. There were no statistical differences. Therefore, we chose to base our present analyses on those with 3 or more TF-CBT sessions as we had done in our previous study. Figure 1 shows the number of children who completed three or more sessions and a post-test K-SADS assessment in each of the treatment conditions. The mean number of TF-CBT sessions attended by the 87 children ... in the two 8 session conditions was 7.36 ... whereas the mean number was 13.92 ... for the 92 children ... in the two 16 session conditions. Of the children/parents assigned to the 8 session conditions, 63 (72%) completed all eight treatment sessions, whereas only 50 (54%) of the children/parents assigned to the 16 session conditions completed all 16 sessions of treatment. A MM-ANCOVA is robust with respect to the occurrence of randomly missing data. Consequently, all of the available data at each evaluation were employed in the mixed model analyses, regardless if a child or parent missed one of the 8 or 16 week evaluations. A modified intent-to-treat approach was also used to assess the impact of missing data upon ANCOVA results. SAS Multiple Imputation and Multiple Imputation Analysis procedures were employed to estimate the missing post-treatment outcome scores and 10 maximum-likelihood pre- and post-treatment complete sets of data were generated for each of the 14 outcome measures based on the initial number of respondents with pre-treatment scores. All of the parameter estimates from the MM-ANCOVAS with the multiple imputation datasets were similar to those that had been found without imputing missing data. Therefore, only the ANCOVA results with the complete sets of data are reported.
evidence.missing_information: Attrition counts and numbers analyzed are not provided by each of the four individual arms in the text (only aggregated: 210 randomized; 31 dropouts; 179 with >=3 sessions; numbers for 8 vs 16 session groups given as combined arms). Figure 1 referenced for per-arm numbers is not provided here. Specific numbers analyzed per outcome measure at each timepoint by arm not reported in text. Reasons for dropout not detailed beyond 'never returned' or 'left after attending only one or two sessions'. Timing of missingness for each outcome measure not fully specified. While multiple imputation was performed, the reported results presented are from completers (>=3 sessions), and exact handling of missing outcome data in primary reported analyses could introduce bias.
evidence.low_risk_signals: Overall dropout reported (31/210 = 15%). Authors performed analyses including all randomized (intent-to-treat) and compared to completer analyses and reported no statistical differences. Mixed-model ANCOVA used and authors state it is robust to randomly missing data. Multiple imputation (SAS MI) with 10 datasets was performed and estimates were similar to analyses without imputation.
evidence.high_risk_signals: Primary reported analyses were restricted to participants with >=3 sessions (completer-based), not strictly ITT; results presented are from complete sets rather than imputed datasets. Per-arm missingness numbers and reasons not fully detailed in text provided; potential differential attrition by arm (e.g., completion of all sessions: 72% in 8-session combined arms vs 54% in 16-session combined arms) suggests differential adherence by length arm.
evidence.outcome_type: All outcomes (self-report, parent-report, clinician interview). Attrition affects all outcomes.
evidence.attrition_by_arm: Reported aggregated: 210 randomized total. 16 (8%) never returned after assignment; 15 (7%) left after 1-2 sessions = 31 dropouts (15%); 179 (85%) attended >=3 sessions and were included in subsequent analyses. Combined two 8-session conditions: 87 children (mean sessions 7.36), combined two 16-session conditions: 92 children (mean sessions 13.92). Of those assigned to 8-session conditions, 63 (72%) completed all 8 sessions; of those assigned to 16-session conditions, 50 (54%) completed all 16 sessions. Per individual arm numbers for completions/post-test not provided in text (Figure 1 referenced). Reasons for dropout: not detailed beyond not returning or leaving after 1-2 sessions.
evidence.external_evidence_needed: Yes — per-arm enrollment, numbers analyzed per outcome and timepoint (from Figure 1, protocol, or trial registry), detailed reasons for dropout by arm, and which dataset was primary (ITT vs completer) for reported tables.

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Sample Figure 1 shows the number of children assessed at various stages of the study. Two hundred ten children, ages 4–11 years old, along with their 164 parents were randomly assigned to one of four treatment conditions: 8 sessions with no TN (8 No TN), 8 sessions with TN (8 Yes TN), 16 sessions with no TN (16 No TN), and 16 sessions with TN (16 Yes TN). The term “parent” describes any primary caregiver who accompanied, entered treatment, and provided the principal information about the child. The difference between the number of parents and children was attributable to 17 (8%) siblings being included in the study. Siblings were randomized to the same condition and the results did not differ as a function of sibling inclusion. The children were recruited from two sites (Pittsburgh, PA and Stratford, NJ). --- ## Sample Children had to have at least five PTSD symptoms, including at...

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Background Venous thromboembolism (VTE) has an incidence of 1-2 per 1000 individuals per year but is close to 1% per annum in those aged over 70 years[1]. VTE is a leading cause of death in hospital inpatients[2] and is a major cause of morbidity and mortality particularly in older people and among those with cancer[1,3]. Despite the fact that 70% of patients with VTE are aged over 60[1], there are few studies of risk factors in the elderly. Other studies have analyzed effects of pre-existing statin medication on incident VTE [4-6] or have compared statin use in case control studies of VTE[7-9]. A post hoc analysis of the Heart and Estrogen/progestin Replacement Study (HERS)[4] and analyses of other[5-8], but not all [9], population or case control studies indicated a decreased risk of VTE with statin use. A systematic review of observational studies suggested that statins may be...

evidence.reported_evidence: Of 108 patients screened, 90 were randomized: 60 to treatment and 30 to control. We excluded three patients < 17 years of age from the analysis... thus, 87 patients were included in the present analysis. Follow-up: Five (8%) patients in the enzyme replacement therapy group and four (14%) patients in the placebo group did not complete the study. Reasons for not completing the study in the treatment group included: hypersensitivity reactions related to the study medication (2), death unrelated to treatment (1), and switch to commercial enzyme replacement therapy (2). In the control group, reasons included: adverse event not related to study medication (1), switch to commercial enzyme replacement therapy (1), and missed 78-week study appointments (2). Of patients with baseline ECG data, 89% had follow-up ECG data. Of patients with baseline echocardiographic data, 80% had follow up echocardiographic data. "Only those patients with data points both at baseline and at 78 weeks were included in the efficacy analysis."
evidence.missing_information: Exact numbers analyzed per outcome (e.g., how many ECGs/echos included in specific analyses by arm), whether analyses were by intention-to-treat, per-protocol, or completer-only; how missing data were handled (imputation, LOCF, sensitivity analyses) beyond statement that only those with both baseline and 78-week data were included; timing and distribution of missingness across other outcomes; whether deaths were included in analyses or censored.
evidence.low_risk_signals: Overall attrition relatively low (5/60 treatment; 4/30 control). Reasons for missingness provided and include specific treatment-related (hypersensitivity) and non-treatment reasons (switch to commercial therapy, missed visits), and death noted as unrelated to treatment. Reporting of percent follow-up for ECG and echo (89% and 80%) provides transparency.
evidence.high_risk_signals: The efficacy analysis included only participants with both baseline and 78-week data (completer analysis), which may introduce bias if missingness is related to outcome or treatment; no ITT analysis reported and no imputation described. Slightly higher percentage dropout in placebo (14% vs 8%) but overall small numbers; reasons include switch to commercial therapy potentially differential. Death in treatment arm not further detailed and handling in analysis not described.
evidence.outcome_type: All outcomes; specifically ECG and echocardiogram (objective) reported here.
evidence.attrition_by_arm: Randomized: treatment 60, control 30. Excluded from present analysis: 3 patients <17 (not specified arm). Did not complete study: treatment 5 (hypersensitivity reactions 2; death unrelated to treatment 1; switch to commercial therapy 2), control 4 (adverse event not related to study medication 1; switch to commercial therapy 1; missed 78-week appointments 2). Follow-up ECG availability: 89% of those with baseline ECG had follow-up. Follow-up echocardiographic availability: 80% of those with baseline echocardiogram had follow-up. Efficacy analysis: "Only those patients with data points both at baseline and at 78 weeks were included."
evidence.external_evidence_needed: Yes — need exact numbers analyzed per outcome by arm, indication of ITT vs per-protocol, methods for handling missing data, and any sensitivity analyses; may be available in primary trial publication or protocol.

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## INTRODUCTION Pompe disease (glycogen storage disease type II; OMIM # 232300) is an autosomal recessive disease caused by a deficiency in the lysosomal enzyme acid alpha-glucosidase. This enzyme defect impacts an estimated 1:40,000 live births and results in an accumulation of glycogen in tissues throughout the body including smooth, skeletal, and cardiac muscle (1,2). There is a wide spectrum of disease severity dependant on the level of residual enzyme activity. Classic infantile Pompe disease is characterized by residual enzyme activity of < 1% and progressive hypotonia, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, hepatomegaly, and respiratory insufficiency typically leading to death within the first year of life without enzyme replacement therapy (3,4). In contrast, late onset Pompe disease (juvenile and adult) is caused by a partial deficiency of acid alpha-glucosidase and has a more insidious...

Reasons: treatment group - hypersensitivity reactions related to the study medication (2), death unrelated to treatment (1), switch to commercial enzyme replacement therapy (2). Control group - adverse event not related to study medication (1), switch to commercial enzyme replacement therapy (1), missed 78-week study appointments (2).
results.external_evidence_needed: Yes — need confirmation from trial protocol/primary publication about who exactly was blinded (participants, clinicians, infusion staff) and blinding maintenance procedures; information on whether unblinding occurred for any participants.

results.domain: Blinding of outcome assessment: All outcomes
results.reported_evidence: "All study electrocardiograms (ECG) and echocardiograms were performed at one of the eight principal study sites (see Appendix A). A 12-lead ECG was obtained at baseline and at 26, 52, and 78 weeks. All ECG’s were interpreted at the central cardiology core lab by a cardiologist blinded to treatment arm." "Echocardiographic evaluation... All echocardiograms were interpreted at the central cardiology core lab by a cardiologist blinded to treatment allocation."
results.missing_information: Blinding status of other outcome assessors (e.g., those performing ECG/echo acquisition at sites, clinicians assessing adverse events, assessors of other outcomes such as 6-minute walk or pulmonary function in original trial) not explicitly reported in this article. No details on blinding of those collecting safety/adverse event data or treating clinicians. No information on whether central readers remained blinded throughout or whether any unblinding occurred.
results.low_risk_signals: Central cardiology core lab readers (for ECG and echocardiogram interpretation) explicitly stated as blinded to treatment allocation — strong signal for low risk for these objective cardiac outcomes.
results.high_risk_signals: Acquisition of ECG/echo at sites could be influenced by unblinded site staff if they were unblinded; no statement that acquisition staff were blinded. Other outcomes' assessors (e.g., for clinical events) not described.
results.outcome_type: Objective (ECG, echocardiogram) — central blinded interpretation; other outcomes not specified here.
results.attrition_by_arm: Of patients with baseline ECG data, 89% had follow-up ECG data. Of patients with baseline echocardiographic data, 80% had follow-up echocardiographic data. Overall completions: 60 randomized to treatment, 30 to control; analysis cohort for cardiovascular analysis excluded 3 patients <17 resulting in 87 included; 5 treatment and 4 placebo did not complete study (reasons provided).
results.external_evidence_needed: No for central reading blinding of ECG/echocardiogram (reported). Yes for blinding of acquisition staff and other outcome assessors — may require protocol or primary trial report.

results.reported_evidence: Of 108 patients screened, 90 were randomized: 60 to treatment and 30 to control. We excluded three patients < 17 years of age from the analysis... thus, 87 patients were included in the present analysis. Follow-up: Five (8%) patients in the enzyme replacement therapy group and four (14%) patients in the placebo group did not complete the study. Reasons for not completing the study in the treatment group included: hypersensitivity reactions related to the study medication (2), death unrelated to treatment (1), and switch to commercial enzyme replacement therapy (2). In the control group, reasons included: adverse event not related to study medication (1), switch to commercial enzyme replacement therapy (1), and missed 78-week study appointments (2). Of patients with baseline ECG data, 89% had follow-up ECG data. Of patients with baseline echocardiographic data, 80% had follow up echocardiographic data. "Only those patients with data points both at baseline and at 78 weeks were included in the efficacy analysis."
results.missing_information: Exact numbers analyzed per outcome (e.g., how many ECGs/echos included in specific analyses by arm), whether analyses were by intention-to-treat, per-protocol, or completer-only; how missing data were handled (imputation, LOCF, sensitivity analyses) beyond statement that only those with both baseline and 78-week data were included; timing and distribution of missingness across other outcomes; whether deaths were included in analyses or censored.
results.low_risk_signals: Overall attrition relatively low (5/60 treatment; 4/30 control). Reasons for missingness provided and include specific treatment-related (hypersensitivity) and non-treatment reasons (switch to commercial therapy, missed visits), and death noted as unrelated to treatment. Reporting of percent follow-up for ECG and echo (89% and 80%) provides transparency.
results.high_risk_signals: The efficacy analysis included only participants with both baseline and 78-week data (completer analysis), which may introduce bias if missingness is related to outcome or treatment; no ITT analysis reported and no imputation described. Slightly higher percentage dropout in placebo (14% vs 8%) but overall small numbers; reasons include switch to commercial therapy potentially differential. Death in treatment arm not further detailed and handling in analysis not described.
results.outcome_type: All outcomes; specifically ECG and echocardiogram (objective) reported here.
results.attrition_by_arm: Randomized: treatment 60, control 30. Excluded from present analysis: 3 patients <17 (not specified arm). Did not complete study: treatment 5 (hypersensitivity reactions 2; death unrelated to treatment 1; switch to commercial therapy 2), control 4 (adverse event not related to study medication 1; switch to commercial therapy 1; missed 78-week appointments 2). Follow-up ECG availability: 89% of those with baseline ECG had follow-up. Follow-up echocardiographic availability: 80% of those with baseline echocardiogram had follow-up. Efficacy analysis: "Only those patients with data points both at baseline and at 78 weeks were included."
results.external_evidence_needed: Yes — need exact numbers analyzed per outcome by arm, indication of ITT vs per-protocol, methods for handling missing data, and any sensitivity analyses; may be available in primary trial publication or protocol.

evidence.reported_evidence: "Among the 191 women who were randomized, 20 were withdrawn from the study (15 of whom were randomized to the COCT group but then withdrawn due to medical contra-indications). In each of the three groups, 86% of participants completed at least one follow-up assessment. In the analysis sample, the three treatment groups did not differ significantly in rates of completion of both follow-up assessments (92% SMT, 98% TSMT, 92% COCT; P = 0.47) ..." "Among the COCT analysis sample, 51% completed the COCT protocol, 33% never started the medication (most because they decided after randomization that they did not want to take the medication), and 16% started but did not complete the medication protocol (most because of side effects). Among the 25 women who completed the COCT protocol, 23 completed the 6-month questionnaire assessment prior to stopping the medication, one did not complete the 6-month assessment, and one completed the questionnaire six days after ending medication use. In sum, among 74 women randomized to the COCT arm of the study, only 25 (34%) completed the entire COC protocol." "We compared the analysis sample (n = 147) to study participants excluded from analysis (i.e., no follow-up data; n = 24) ... There were no significant (P < 0.05) differences in treatment group assignment..." "The primary analyses ... were intent-to-treat analyses, which included all randomized participants for whom follow-up data (either at 6 months or 12 months) were available..." "we performed multiple imputation analyses for the outcomes of characteristic pain intensity and activity inference to assess the effect of missing data due to participants who provided no follow-up data... used a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) method ... to create five imputations ..."
evidence.missing_information: Exact numbers randomized per arm not explicitly restated here (though COCT n=74, SMT n=60, TSMT n=57 implied elsewhere); precise counts of analyzed participants at each timepoint by arm beyond percentages not fully tabulated in the provided text. Details of reasons for all missing data across arms other than COCT withdrawals are limited. How withdrawals prior to starting treatment (e.g., 15 COCT withdrawn for medical contraindications) were handled in analyses (excluded before randomization or after?) is somewhat described but could be clearer.
evidence.low_risk_signals: Primary analyses were intent-to-treat including all randomized participants with any follow-up; authors performed multiple imputation for key outcomes and sensitivity analyses comparing completers/as-treated. Comparison showed no significant differences between analysis sample (n=147) and those with no follow-up (n=24) on key baseline variables.
evidence.high_risk_signals: High and differential non-adherence in COCT arm: 33% never started medication and only 34% completed entire COCT protocol; 15 randomized to COCT were withdrawn due to medical contraindications after randomization. 20 randomized were withdrawn overall. These factors create potential for bias if not adequately handled, and large proportion of missing/withdrew in COCT arm.
evidence.outcome_type: All outcomes (primary outcomes are self-reported pain and activity interference)
evidence.attrition_by_arm: Reported: overall randomized = 191; withdrawn = 20 (15 from COCT for medical contraindications). Analysis sample n=147, excluded (no follow-up) n=24. Completion of both follow-ups: 92% SMT, 98% TSMT, 92% COCT. COCT specifics: of 74 randomized to COCT, 25 (34%) completed entire protocol; 33% never started medication; 16% started but did not complete. Exact numbers analyzed per arm at each timepoint not fully tabulated in provided text.
evidence.external_evidence_needed: No

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## 2.1. Setting and participants Study participants were recruited from patients seeking care at the U.W. Orofacial Pain Clinic and by advertising between October 2005 and June 2009. Study inclusion criteria were: (1) female gender; (2) age 18–45 years; (3) a Research Diagnostic Criteria/Temporomandibular Disorders (RDC/TMD) Axis I TMD pain diagnosis [12] made by an oral medicine specialist based on a structured RDC/TMD clinical examination; (4) premenopausal; (5) characteristic pain intensity [55] ≥3 (0–10 scale, past 6 months timeframe); and (6) ability to communicate in English. Study exclusion criteria were lacking a menstrual cycle; pregnant, lactating, or planning to become pregnant in the next 7 months; unwilling to take a continuous OC; need for further diagnostic evaluation of facial pain (as determined by the oral medicine specialist); and major medical or psychiatric...

results.reported_evidence: "Among the 191 women who were randomized, 20 were withdrawn from the study (15 of whom were randomized to the COCT group but then withdrawn due to medical contra-indications). In each of the three groups, 86% of participants completed at least one follow-up assessment. In the analysis sample, the three treatment groups did not differ significantly in rates of completion of both follow-up assessments (92% SMT, 98% TSMT, 92% COCT; P = 0.47) ..." "Among the COCT analysis sample, 51% completed the COCT protocol, 33% never started the medication (most because they decided after randomization that they did not want to take the medication), and 16% started but did not complete the medication protocol (most because of side effects). Among the 25 women who completed the COCT protocol, 23 completed the 6-month questionnaire assessment prior to stopping the medication, one did not complete the 6-month assessment, and one completed the questionnaire six days after ending medication use. In sum, among 74 women randomized to the COCT arm of the study, only 25 (34%) completed the entire COC protocol." "We compared the analysis sample (n = 147) to study participants excluded from analysis (i.e., no follow-up data; n = 24) ... There were no significant (P < 0.05) differences in treatment group assignment..." "The primary analyses ... were intent-to-treat analyses, which included all randomized participants for whom follow-up data (either at 6 months or 12 months) were available..." "we performed multiple imputation analyses for the outcomes of characteristic pain intensity and activity inference to assess the effect of missing data due to participants who provided no follow-up data... used a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) method ... to create five imputations ..."
results.missing_information: Exact numbers randomized per arm not explicitly restated here (though COCT n=74, SMT n=60, TSMT n=57 implied elsewhere); precise counts of analyzed participants at each timepoint by arm beyond percentages not fully tabulated in the provided text. Details of reasons for all missing data across arms other than COCT withdrawals are limited. How withdrawals prior to starting treatment (e.g., 15 COCT withdrawn for medical contraindications) were handled in analyses (excluded before randomization or after?) is somewhat described but could be clearer.
results.low_risk_signals: Primary analyses were intent-to-treat including all randomized participants with any follow-up; authors performed multiple imputation for key outcomes and sensitivity analyses comparing completers/as-treated. Comparison showed no significant differences between analysis sample (n=147) and those with no follow-up (n=24) on key baseline variables.
results.high_risk_signals: High and differential non-adherence in COCT arm: 33% never started medication and only 34% completed entire COCT protocol; 15 randomized to COCT were withdrawn due to medical contraindications after randomization. 20 randomized were withdrawn overall. These factors create potential for bias if not adequately handled, and large proportion of missing/withdrew in COCT arm.
results.outcome_type: All outcomes (primary outcomes are self-reported pain and activity interference)
results.attrition_by_arm: Reported: overall randomized = 191; withdrawn = 20 (15 from COCT for medical contraindications). Analysis sample n=147, excluded (no follow-up) n=24. Completion of both follow-ups: 92% SMT, 98% TSMT, 92% COCT. COCT specifics: of 74 randomized to COCT, 25 (34%) completed entire protocol; 33% never started medication; 16% started but did not complete. Exact numbers analyzed per arm at each timepoint not fully tabulated in provided text.
results.external_evidence_needed: No

evidence.reported_evidence: Randomized: 88. "Sixteen (eight in each group) did not complete baseline paperwork and were not exposed to treatment." Treatment-exposed: n=33 in MT, n=38 in FFS. "The six, 12, and 17-week follow-up completion rates were 27 (82% of treatment-exposed individuals) and 33 (87%), 29 (88%) and 32 (84%), and 29 (88%) and 33 (87%) for MT and FFS respectively." One individual incarcerated after treatment was excluded from analyses per VA regulations. "Longitudinal data were analyzed using intent-to-treat mixed effect regression models on the full sample of randomized subjects (minus one individual who was incarcerated after treatment and whose data were not allowed to be analyzed per Veteran’s Administration regulations) ... Incomplete data were handled using casewise deletion, using all available data for parameter estimates (Hedeker et al., 2007)." Also: of 244 CO measurements, eight (3.3%) unverified due to moving, two (0.8%) confounded by marijuana use, one (0.4%) unverified due to RA error, and one (0.4%) inconsistent with self-report and counted as non-abstinent.
evidence.missing_information: Exact numbers analyzed per outcome at each time point by original randomized arm (i.e., whether analyses included the 16 who did not start treatment); whether primary abstinence outcomes used missing=smoking or other imputation; details on how casewise deletion was applied in mixed models; whether LOCF or other imputations were used; reasons for dropouts per arm beyond the 16 baseline non-starters and the one incarcerated.
evidence.low_risk_signals: Follow-up completion rates reported by arm and timepoint; use of mixed effect regression models on full sample (minus one incarcerated individual) suggests use of all available data in longitudinal analyses.
evidence.high_risk_signals: Sixteen randomized participants (8 per arm) did not start treatment and were not exposed; unclear whether included in ITT analyses—text says mixed models on full sample minus one incarcerated but also says casewise deletion used for incomplete data; potential exclusion of randomized participants and use of casewise deletion could bias results. One participant excluded due to incarceration per VA regs.
evidence.outcome_type: All (self-reported abstinence and cigarettes/day; CO objective verification for abstinence).
evidence.attrition_by_arm: Randomized: 88. Not exposed to treatment: 16 (8 MT, 8 FFS). Treatment-exposed: MT n=33, FFS n=38. Follow-up completion at 6 weeks: MT 27 (82% of treatment-exposed), FFS 33 (87%). 12 weeks: MT 29 (88%), FFS 32 (84%). 17 weeks: MT 29 (88%), FFS 33 (87%). results.reported_evidence: Randomized: 88. "Sixteen (eight in each group) did not complete baseline paperwork and were not exposed to treatment." Treatment-exposed: n=33 in MT, n=38 in FFS. "The six, 12, and 17-week follow-up completion rates were 27 (82% of treatment-exposed individuals) and 33 (87%), 29 (88%) and 32 (84%), and 29 (88%) and 33 (87%) for MT and FFS respectively." One individual incarcerated after treatment was excluded from analyses per VA regulations. "Longitudinal data were analyzed using intent-to-treat mixed effect regression models on the full sample of randomized subjects (minus one individual who was incarcerated after treatment and whose data were not allowed to be analyzed per Veteran’s Administration regulations) ... Incomplete data were handled using casewise deletion, using all available data for parameter estimates (Hedeker et al., 2007)." Also: of 244 CO measurements, eight (3.3%) unverified due to moving, two (0.8%) confounded by marijuana use, one (0.4%) unverified due to RA error, and one (0.4%) inconsistent with self-report and counted as non-abstinent.
results.missing_information: Exact numbers analyzed per outcome at each time point by original randomized arm (i.e., whether analyses included the 16 who did not start treatment); whether primary abstinence outcomes used missing=smoking or other imputation; details on how casewise deletion was applied in mixed models; whether LOCF or other imputations were used; reasons for dropouts per arm beyond the 16 baseline non-starters and the one incarcerated.
results.low_risk_signals: Follow-up completion rates reported by arm and timepoint; use of mixed effect regression models on full sample (minus one incarcerated individual) suggests use of all available data in longitudinal analyses.
results.high_risk_signals: Sixteen randomized participants (8 per arm) did not start treatment and were not exposed; unclear whether included in ITT analyses—text says mixed models on full sample minus one incarcerated but also says casewise deletion used for incomplete data; potential exclusion of randomized participants and use of casewise deletion could bias results. One participant excluded due to incarceration per VA regs.
results.outcome_type: All (self-reported abstinence and cigarettes/day; CO objective verification for abstinence).

ground_truth.support_text: More than 40% of participants discontinued intervention in both groups during the 4-year study. We do not know how many participants were lost before the outcomes at 2 years (our outcome of interest), and how many after the first 2 years. Most participants (454/460) were included in the ITT analysis, but the study authors did not report for how many they managed to measure the outcome, and for how many they used the last observation carried forward ("mean of weeks 191 and 192 or last available value after randomization"). Apparently, the proportion of dropouts and the causes of dropout were equally distributed among the 2 groups.

ground_truth_support: More than 40% of participants discontinued intervention in both groups during the 4-year study. We do not know how many participants were lost before the outcomes at 2 years (our outcome of interest), and how many after the first 2 years. Most participants (454/460) were included in the ITT analysis, but the study authors did not report for how many they managed to measure the outcome, and for how many they used the last observation carried forward ("mean of weeks 191 and 192 or last available value after randomization"). Apparently, the proportion of dropouts and the causes of dropout were equally distributed among the 2 groups.

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Front Diabetic distal symmetric sensorimotor polyneuropathy (DSPN) is a chronic progressive disease affecting around one-third of the diabetic population and accounts for considerable morbidity, increased mortality, and reduced quality of life (1,2). Recent long-term studies in type 2 diabetic patients indicate that the current strategies of intensive diabetes therapy or multifactorial cardiovascular risk intervention are not sufficient to slow the progression of DSPN (3–5). Thus, effective treatment of DSPN remains challenging for the physician (1,6). --- ## Front Based on the pathogenetic mechanisms of DSPN, potential disease-modifying therapeutic approaches have been developed including antioxidants such as α-lipoic acid (ALA) (7–9) to diminish increased oxidative stress (10). Other potential modalities include the aldose reductase inhibitors (11), growth factors (12), and the...

results.reported_evidence: "The investigators and the monitor received sealed envelopes to enable decoding the individual blinded treatment in case of emergency." results.high_risk_signals: Sealed envelopes mentioned only for emergency decoding; no description of procedures used to conceal allocation prior to patient enrollment/randomization (e.g., opaque sealed sequential envelopes or central telephone/web randomization) — important details absent.

evidence.reported_evidence: Sample size and anticipated dropout reported: "a dropout rate of 20% during the study is expected... Therefore, 2 × 140 participants are needed to enrol in the trial." "Non-compliant participants of the programme group are approached for all follow-up measurements, and participants with missing data are contacted to ensure completion of data, as recommended by Hollis and Campbell." Analysis plan: "Data of the effect evaluation will be analysed according to both the intention-to-treat and per-protocol principles. In the first analyses all participants will be included according to their original assignment [52]. Participants of the intervention group who attended at least five of the seven programme sessions will be included in the per-protocol analysis." No actual participant flow, numbers randomized, numbers analysed, or missing outcome counts or reasons are provided in the text supplied.
