trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Background One of the programmes with proven effectiveness is the Dutch version of 'A Matter of Balance' (AMB-NL) [20]. This multicomponent cognitive behavioural group programme consists of eight weekly group sessions and a booster session after six months. It has shown favourable effects on concerns about falls, perceived control over falling and daily activity, after at least 8 months of follow-up. In addition, significantly fewer recurrent fallers were observed in this group after 14 months of follow-up [15]. Notwithstanding these positive outcomes, approximately 40% of the participants attended less than five sessions out of a total eight, mainly because of health problems [21]. It seems that, in particular, frail older people refrained from attending the group programme. This is also seen in other group programmes that explicitly address concerns about falls, and target frail...

evidence_table.study_design: Two-group randomised controlled trial with baseline and follow-ups at 5 and 12 months; stratified block randomisation performed by an external agency.
evidence_table.notes: All extracted evidence comes from the provided article text. No review-level context, registry, protocol, or author correspondence beyond the article text was used.

results.reported_evidence: "stratified randomisation. This randomisation was performed in blocks of two on the basis of one prognostic factor: the level of concern about falls (some, regular, often, and very often). Computerised alternative allocation was used in the randomisation process, which was performed by an external agency."
results.missing_information: Exact method for generating the random sequence (e.g. random number generator algorithm, software name, seed) not reported; whether allocation was truly random (vs. quasi-random) beyond 'computerised' not specified; block randomisation details (e.g. fixed vs variable block size) not reported.
results.low_risk_signals: Use of computerised allocation and an external agency suggests centrally generated random sequence rather than manual methods.
results.high_risk_signals: Blocks of two is a small fixed block size which can allow predictability if allocation concealment is inadequate (note: predictability depends on concealment details which are not reported).
results.outcome_type: Not directly applicable
results.external_evidence_needed: No; article states computerised allocation by external agency but lacks technical details — further protocol/registry or author clarification would be needed to confirm specifics.

results.reported_evidence: "Computerised alternative allocation was used in the randomisation process, which was performed by an external agency."
results.missing_information: No explicit description of who controlled assignment before enrolment; no statement of central randomisation procedures at time of enrolment; no mention of pharmacy-control, opaque sealed envelopes, independent allocation office, telephone/online central randomisation, or other concealment mechanism; timing relative to enrolment not fully detailed beyond 'directly after the baseline measurement'.
results.low_risk_signals: Allocation was performed by an external agency which may imply independent allocation and potential adequate concealment.
results.high_risk_signals: No explicit methods described to ensure concealment (e.g. central telephone/online randomisation or sealed envelopes); hence potential for selection bias if allocation could be predicted or manipulated.
results.outcome_type: Not directly applicable
results.external_evidence_needed: Yes; article is ambiguous about the operational procedures used to conceal allocation — protocol, registry entry, or author clarification required to confirm concealment method.

results.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel: All outcomes
results.reported_evidence: "Data for the effect evaluation is gathered by means of telephone interviews which are conducted by trained interviewers, who are blinded for group allocation." No other statements about blinding of participants or intervention personnel (facilitators) provided. Intervention described as an in-home programme facilitated by trained nurses; control is a no-treatment control group.
results.missing_information: Whether participants were informed of their group assignment (likely yes given active in-home visits) not explicitly stated; whether facilitators/intervention personnel were blinded clearly not possible and not reported; no mention of attempts to blind participants (e.g. sham visits) or to blind personnel delivering intervention; no information on whether participants knew there was a no-treatment control or any expectation management.
results.low_risk_signals: Trained interviewers conducting outcome data collection were blinded to group allocation—relevant for participant-reported outcomes collected by interview.
results.high_risk_signals: Intervention is behavioural in-home programme delivered by nurses, making blinding of participants and personnel unlikely; no report of any participant or personnel blinding procedures.
results.outcome_type: Relevant outcomes include self-reported measures (FES-I, FES-IAB, GARS, HADS, etc.), and objectively recorded falls via participant-completed calendars (self-reported).
results.external_evidence_needed: No for interviewer blinding (reported). Yes for participant and personnel blinding — article does not report and clarification would be needed to document attempts or impossibility of blinding.
